summary:
Claim: The permanent lower central incisor has the unique property among anterior teeth of having a mesiodistal width exceeding its cervical-to-incisal height.  
Evidence: - In permanent dentition, the lower central incisor shows wider mesiodistal dimensions compared to its vertical height. - This characteristic distinguishes it from other anterior teeth like lateral incisors and deciduous teeth which typically have taller cervical-to-incisal heights relative to their widths.  
Answer: B

The claim is that the permanent lower central incisor is the only anterior tooth exhibiting a mesiodistal width greater than its cervico-incisal dimension. Key evidence includes anatomical data showing that the permanent lower central incisor has a broader crown width than its vertical height—unlike most other anterior teeth—which usually exhibit greater cervical-to-incisal height. This distinction is consistent across standard dental references and highlights functional adaptations related to occlusal function. Given these findings, the correct choice based on established dental morphology is option B.
